title: Going back to first principles, on purpose
excerpt: Six years shipping production agentic systems taught me how to build with LLMs. Now I'm going back through the fundamentals properly, in the open.
body:
## Why write in the open

Most of my technical growth over the last few years has come from shipping — multi-agent platforms, retrieval pipelines, fine-tuned classifiers running in production. That's real experience, but it's a specific kind: applied engineering optimises for *what works in production*, which isn't always the same muscle as understanding *why* something works from first principles.

I got properly interested in this during a university AI module that went from the fundamentals through CNNs and transformers — that's what pulled me toward this field in the first place. Since then, the applied side has moved fast, and I want to go back through the fundamentals deliberately: not because I'm starting from zero, but because the gap between "I studied this" and "I could rebuild it and explain every decision" is worth closing properly, especially with how quickly the field's moved on since.

This is the first post in a series doing exactly that — working back through the fundamentals (attention from scratch, policy gradient methods, reward modelling) and writing up what I learn, including the parts I get wrong the first time.

### What's next

- A transformer implemented from scratch, no framework shortcuts
- Notes on PPO vs DPO for preference optimisation
- Reward modelling experiments on small open datasets

If you're doing something similar — refreshing and deepening fundamentals alongside applied work — I'd like to hear what worked for you.

title: Using contextual bandits to make RAG systems adaptive
excerpt: Exploring how reinforcement learning techniques can help retrieval systems adapt as query patterns change — and the challenges that come with feedback-driven optimisation.
body:
## The problem

Static RAG configurations can degrade as query patterns change over time. Retrieval strategies that work well for one group of queries may become less effective as requirements evolve, making continuous evaluation and adaptation important.

One approach I have been exploring is using contextual bandits to help systems select between different retrieval and generation strategies based on feedback signals. By using time-weighted rewards, newer observations can have more influence than older ones when measuring performance.

### Challenges with feedback-driven optimisation

- **Reward attribution lag.** Feedback often arrives after an interaction, making it challenging to correctly associate outcomes with the actions that produced them.
- **Cold-start variance.** New strategies require exploration before enough signal exists to understand their effectiveness.
- **Reward model drift.** When evaluation relies on models such as LLM-based judges, the evaluation process itself needs monitoring to ensure optimisation targets the right behaviours.

Adaptive AI systems introduce interesting optimisation challenges and provide opportunities for building more robust retrieval pipelines.
